critic_raw:
This design compares a bidirectional LSTM using sequential visit history against logistic regression using aggregated features for 30-day readmission prediction.

The primary evaluation metric is 'Recall at 80% precision.' The 80% precision threshold is determined on the validation set, and the design explicitly states this threshold is used to evaluate both models on the test set. Precision depends on both the model's discriminative capacity and the positive class prevalence. If readmission prevalence in the test set (months 31–36) differs from the validation set (months 25–30) — a realistic scenario given seasonal and care-practice variation — the threshold calibrated to achieve 80% precision on validation will produce a different precision on the test set. This means the comparison of 'recall at 80% precision' will be conducted at a threshold that does not actually achieve 80% precision for either model on the test set, making the primary metric comparison internally invalid.

The bidirectional LSTM uses left-padding to align sequences to a maximum length of 50 visits. Left-padding means the first elements of shorter sequences are zeros, and the LSTM reads from left to right through the padding before reaching actual clinical events. In the reverse (backward) direction, the LSTM reads from the end of the sequence backward — which starts at the most recent visit for sequences without padding. This asymmetry means the backward direction gets a clean temporal signal while the forward direction must learn to ignore padding. The choice of left-padding vs. right-padding has architectural consequences that are not acknowledged.

The baseline logistic regression's hyperparameter C is described as tuned via '5-fold cross-validation nested within the training set on validation recall.' Performing 5-fold CV within the training set to tune the baseline's C, while the LSTM is tuned on the validation set using early stopping and threshold selection, uses different optimization protocols for the two models. The baseline gets more internal tuning (nested CV) while the LSTM gets validation-set-guided tuning with additional degrees of freedom (epoch selection AND threshold selection). These are not equivalent tuning procedures, and the difference in C selection methodology may itself affect the baseline's calibration on the test set.
all_issues_raised: The precision threshold calibrated to achieve 80% precision on the validation set may not produce 80% precision on the test set if readmission prevalence shifts between the two time windows, making 'Recall at 80% precision' an ill-defined test-set metric.

Left-padding creates an asymmetry in the bidirectional LSTM: the backward direction reads from the most recent visit (useful signal) while the forward direction must traverse padding before reaching clinical events — this is an unacknowledged architectural choice with performance implications.

The baseline logistic regression uses nested 5-fold CV for hyperparameter tuning while the LSTM uses validation-set early stopping plus threshold selection — these are non-equivalent optimization procedures that may introduce different amounts of overfitting to each model.
all_issues_adjudicated: The precision threshold calibrated to achieve 80% precision on the validation set may not produce 80% precision on the test set if readmission prevalence shifts between the two time windows, making 'Recall at 80% precision' an ill-defined test-set metric.

Left-padding creates an asymmetry in the bidirectional LSTM: the backward direction reads from the most recent visit (useful signal) while the forward direction must traverse padding before reaching clinical events — this is an unacknowledged architectural choice with performance implications.

The baseline logistic regression uses nested 5-fold CV for hyperparameter tuning while the LSTM uses validation-set early stopping plus threshold selection — these are non-equivalent optimization procedures that may introduce different amounts of overfitting to each model.
